title: Exponents & Logarithms
blurb: From b^x = N to the domain traps of log inequalities — every exponential form the exam knows.

forms.title: Evaluating logarithms
forms.skill: log_b N = the exponent that turns b into N.

variants.explanation: $\log_{7} 7$ asks: $7$ to what power gives $7$? Since $7^{1} = 7$, the answer is $1$.
$\log_{5} 1$ asks: $5$ to what power gives $1$? Since $5^{0} = 1$, the answer is $0$.
$\log_{2} 4$ asks: $2$ to what power gives $4$? Since $2^{2} = 4$, the answer is $2$.
$\log_{2} 8$ asks: $2$ to what power gives $8$? Since $2^{3} = 8$, the answer is $3$.
$\log_{2} 16$ asks: $2$ to what power gives $16$? Since $2^{4} = 16$, the answer is $4$.
$\log_{2} 32$ asks: $2$ to what power gives $32$? Since $2^{5} = 32$, the answer is $5$.
$\log_{3} 9$ asks: $3$ to what power gives $9$? Since $3^{2} = 9$, the answer is $2$.
$\log_{3} 27$ asks: $3$ to what power gives $27$? Since $3^{3} = 27$, the answer is $3$.
$\log_{3} 81$ asks: $3$ to what power gives $81$? Since $3^{4} = 81$, the answer is $4$.
$\log_{3} 243$ asks: $3$ to what power gives $243$? Since $3^{5} = 243$, the answer is $5$.
$\log_{5} 25$ asks: $5$ to what power gives $25$? Since $5^{2} = 25$, the answer is $2$.
$\log_{5} 125$ asks: $5$ to what power gives $125$? Since $5^{3} = 125$, the answer is $3$.
$\log_{5} 625$ asks: $5$ to what power gives $625$? Since $5^{4} = 625$, the answer is $4$.
$\log_{5} 3125$ asks: $5$ to what power gives $3125$? Since $5^{5} = 3125$, the answer is $5$.
$\log_{7} 49$ asks: $7$ to what power gives $49$? Since $7^{2} = 49$, the answer is $2$.
$\log_{7} 343$ asks: $7$ to what power gives $343$? Since $7^{3} = 343$, the answer is $3$.
$\log_{7} 2401$ asks: $7$ to what power gives $2401$? Since $7^{4} = 2401$, the answer is $4$.
$\log_{7} 16807$ asks: $7$ to what power gives $16807$? Since $7^{5} = 16807$, the answer is $5$.
$\log_{10} 100$ asks: $10$ to what power gives $100$? Since $10^{2} = 100$, the answer is $2$.
$\log_{10} 1000$ asks: $10$ to what power gives $1000$? Since $10^{3} = 1000$, the answer is $3$.
$\log_{10} 10000$ asks: $10$ to what power gives $10000$? Since $10^{4} = 10000$, the answer is $4$.
$\log_{10} 100000$ asks: $10$ to what power gives $100000$? Since $10^{5} = 100000$, the answer is $5$.
$\log_{4} 16$ asks: $4$ to what power gives $16$? Since $4^{2} = 16$, the answer is $2$.
$\log_{4} 64$ asks: $4$ to what power gives $64$? Since $4^{3} = 64$, the answer is $3$.
$\log_{4} 256$ asks: $4$ to what power gives $256$? Since $4^{4} = 256$, the answer is $4$.
$\log_{4} 1024$ asks: $4$ to what power gives $1024$? Since $4^{5} = 1024$, the answer is $5$.
$\log_{6} 36$ asks: $6$ to what power gives $36$? Since $6^{2} = 36$, the answer is $2$.
$\log_{6} 216$ asks: $6$ to what power gives $216$? Since $6^{3} = 216$, the answer is $3$.
$\log_{6} 1296$ asks: $6$ to what power gives $1296$? Since $6^{4} = 1296$, the answer is $4$.
$\log_{6} 7776$ asks: $6$ to what power gives $7776$? Since $6^{5} = 7776$, the answer is $5$.
$\log_{2} 64$ asks: $2$ to what power gives $64$? Since $2^{6} = 64$, the answer is $6$.
$\log_{2} 128$ asks: $2$ to what power gives $128$? Since $2^{7} = 128$, the answer is $7$.
$\log_{2} 256$ asks: $2$ to what power gives $256$? Since $2^{8} = 256$, the answer is $8$.

forms.title: Exponential equations
forms.skill: Match the bases, then equate exponents.

variants.explanation: Write $4$ as a power of $2$: $4 = 2^{2}$. Equal bases $\Rightarrow$ equal exponents: $x = 2$, so $x = 2$.
Write $4$ as a power of $2$: $4 = 2^{2}$. Equal bases $\Rightarrow$ equal exponents: $x + 1 = 2$, so $x = 1$.
Write $4$ as a power of $2$: $4 = 2^{2}$. Equal bases $\Rightarrow$ equal exponents: $x - 1 = 2$, so $x = 3$.

forms.title: Adding logarithms
forms.skill: log M + log N = log(MN) — same base only.

variants.explanation: Same base: logs ADD by multiplying arguments: $\log_{6}(2 \cdot 18) = \log_{6} 36 = 2$, since $6^{2} = 36$.
Same base: logs ADD by multiplying arguments: $\log_{6}(3 \cdot 12) = \log_{6} 36 = 2$, since $6^{2} = 36$.

forms.title: Exponential equations via substitution
forms.skill: b^(2x) is (b^x)² — substitute t = b^x and solve the quadratic.

variants.statement: The equation $2^{2x} - 3 \cdot 2^{x} + 2 = 0$ has two roots. Find their SUM.
variants.explanation: Let $t = 2^{x}$: $t^2 - 3t + 2 = 0$ gives $t = 1$ or $t = 2$, so $2^{x} = 1$ ($x = 0$) or $2^{x} = 2$ ($x = 1$). The sum of the roots is $1$.

variants.statement: The equation $2^{2x} - 5 \cdot 2^{x} + 4 = 0$ has two roots. Find their SUM.
variants.explanation: Let $t = 2^{x}$: $t^2 - 5t + 4 = 0$ gives $t = 1$ or $t = 4$, so $2^{x} = 1$ ($x = 0$) or $2^{x} = 4$ ($x = 2$). The sum of the roots is $2$.

variants.statement: The equation $2^{2x} - 9 \cdot 2^{x} + 8 = 0$ has two roots. Find their SUM.
variants.explanation: Let $t = 2^{x}$: $t^2 - 9t + 8 = 0$ gives $t = 1$ or $t = 8$, so $2^{x} = 1$ ($x = 0$) or $2^{x} = 8$ ($x = 3$). The sum of the roots is $3$.

variants.statement: The equation $2^{2x} - 17 \cdot 2^{x} + 16 = 0$ has two roots. Find their SUM.
The equation $2^{2x} - 6 \cdot 2^{x} + 8 = 0$ has two roots. Find their SUM.
variants.explanation: Let $t = 2^{x}$: $t^2 - 6t + 8 = 0$ gives $t = 2$ or $t = 4$, so $2^{x} = 2$ ($x = 1$) or $2^{x} = 4$ ($x = 2$). The sum of the roots is $3$.

variants.statement: The equation $2^{2x} - 10 \cdot 2^{x} + 16 = 0$ has two roots. Find their SUM.
variants.explanation: Let $t = 2^{x}$: $t^2 - 10t + 16 = 0$ gives $t = 2$ or $t = 8$, so $2^{x} = 2$ ($x = 1$) or $2^{x} = 8$ ($x = 3$). The sum of the roots is $4$.

variants.statement: The equation $3^{2x} - 12 \cdot 3^{x} + 27 = 0$ has two roots. Find their PRODUCT.
variants.explanation: Let $t = 3^{x}$: $t^2 - 12t + 27 = 0$ gives $t = 3$ or $t = 9$, so $3^{x} = 3$ ($x = 1$) or $3^{x} = 9$ ($x = 2$). The product of the roots is $2$.

forms.title: Logarithmic equations
forms.skill: log_b(…) = c means (…) = b^c; check the argument stays positive.

variants.explanation: $\log_{2}(x + 1) = 2$ means $x + 1 = 2^{2} = 4$, so $x = 3$. The argument $x + 1 = 4 > 0$, valid.
$\log_{2}(x + 2) = 2$ means $x + 2 = 2^{2} = 4$, so $x = 2$. The argument $x + 2 = 4 > 0$, valid.
$\log_{2}(x + 3) = 2$ means $x + 3 = 2^{2} = 4$, so $x = 1$. The argument $x + 3 = 4 > 0$, valid.
$\log_{2}(x - 1) = 2$ means $x - 1 = 2^{2} = 4$, so $x = 5$. The argument $x - 1 = 4 > 0$, valid.
$\log_{2}(x - 2) = 2$ means $x - 2 = 2^{2} = 4$, so $x = 6$. The argument $x - 2 = 4 > 0$, valid.

$\log_{3}(x + 1) = 2$ means $x + 1 = 3^{2} = 9$, so $x = 8$. The argument $x + 1 = 9 > 0$, valid.
$\log_{3}(x + 2) = 2$ means $x + 2 = 3^{2} = 9$, so $x = 7$. The argument $x + 2 = 9 > 0$, valid.

$\log_{3}(x - 1) = 2$ means $x - 1 = 3^{2} = 9$, so $x = 10$. The argument $x - 1 = 9 > 0$, valid.

$\log_{3}(x - 3) = 3$ means $x - 3 = 3^{3} = 27$, so $x = 30$. The argument $x - 3 = 27 > 0$, valid.
$\log_{3}(x + 1) = 4$ means $x + 1 = 3^{4} = 81$, so $x = 80$. The argument $x + 1 = 81 > 0$, valid.
$\log_{3}(x + 2) = 4$ means $x + 2 = 3^{4} = 81$, so $x = 79$. The argument $x + 2 = 81 > 0$, valid.

$\log_{3}(x - 1) = 4$ means $x - 1 = 3^{4} = 81$, so $x = 82$. The argument $x - 1 = 81 > 0$, valid.
$\log_{3}(x - 2) = 4$ means $x - 2 = 3^{4} = 81$, so $x = 83$. The argument $x - 2 = 81 > 0$, valid.
$\log_{3}(x - 3) = 4$ means $x - 3 = 3^{4} = 81$, so $x = 84$. The argument $x - 3 = 81 > 0$, valid.

forms.title: Log inequalities (domain trap)
forms.skill: Solve the inequality AND keep the argument positive — intersect both.

variants.explanation: $\log_{2}(x - 1) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 1 < 2^{2} = 4$. Intersecting $x - 1 > 0$ with $x - 1 < 4$ gives $1 < x < 5$.
$\log_{2}(x - 2) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 2 < 2^{2} = 4$. Intersecting $x - 2 > 0$ with $x - 2 < 4$ gives $2 < x < 6$.
$\log_{2}(x - 3) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 3 < 2^{2} = 4$. Intersecting $x - 3 > 0$ with $x - 3 < 4$ gives $3 < x < 7$.
$\log_{2}(x + 1) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x + 1 < 2^{2} = 4$. Intersecting $x + 1 > 0$ with $x + 1 < 4$ gives $-1 < x < 3$.
$\log_{2}(x + 2) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x + 2 < 2^{2} = 4$. Intersecting $x + 2 > 0$ with $x + 2 < 4$ gives $-2 < x < 2$.
$\log_{2}(x - 1) < 3$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 1 < 2^{3} = 8$. Intersecting $x - 1 > 0$ with $x - 1 < 8$ gives $1 < x < 9$.
$\log_{2}(x - 2) < 3$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 2 < 2^{3} = 8$. Intersecting $x - 2 > 0$ with $x - 2 < 8$ gives $2 < x < 10$.
$\log_{2}(x - 3) < 3$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 3 < 2^{3} = 8$. Intersecting $x - 3 > 0$ with $x - 3 < 8$ gives $3 < x < 11$.
$\log_{2}(x + 1) < 3$ needs the argument positive AND $x + 1 < 2^{3} = 8$. Intersecting $x + 1 > 0$ with $x + 1 < 8$ gives $-1 < x < 7$.
$\log_{2}(x + 2) < 3$ needs the argument positive AND $x + 2 < 2^{3} = 8$. Intersecting $x + 2 > 0$ with $x + 2 < 8$ gives $-2 < x < 6$.
$\log_{3}(x - 1) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 1 < 3^{2} = 9$. Intersecting $x - 1 > 0$ with $x - 1 < 9$ gives $1 < x < 10$.
$\log_{3}(x - 2) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 2 < 3^{2} = 9$. Intersecting $x - 2 > 0$ with $x - 2 < 9$ gives $2 < x < 11$.
$\log_{3}(x - 3) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 3 < 3^{2} = 9$. Intersecting $x - 3 > 0$ with $x - 3 < 9$ gives $3 < x < 12$.
$\log_{3}(x + 1) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x + 1 < 3^{2} = 9$. Intersecting $x + 1 > 0$ with $x + 1 < 9$ gives $-1 < x < 8$.
$\log_{3}(x + 2) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x + 2 < 3^{2} = 9$. Intersecting $x + 2 > 0$ with $x + 2 < 9$ gives $-2 < x < 7$.
$\log_{3}(x - 1) < 3$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 1 < 3^{3} = 27$. Intersecting $x - 1 > 0$ with $x - 1 < 27$ gives $1 < x < 28$.
$\log_{3}(x - 2) < 3$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 2 < 3^{3} = 27$. Intersecting $x - 2 > 0$ with $x - 2 < 27$ gives $2 < x < 29$.
$\log_{3}(x - 3) < 3$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 3 < 3^{3} = 27$. Intersecting $x - 3 > 0$ with $x - 3 < 27$ gives $3 < x < 30$.
$\log_{3}(x + 1) < 3$ needs the argument positive AND $x + 1 < 3^{3} = 27$. Intersecting $x + 1 > 0$ with $x + 1 < 27$ gives $-1 < x < 26$.
$\log_{3}(x + 2) < 3$ needs the argument positive AND $x + 2 < 3^{3} = 27$. Intersecting $x + 2 > 0$ with $x + 2 < 27$ gives $-2 < x < 25$.
$\log_{5}(x - 1) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 1 < 5^{2} = 25$. Intersecting $x - 1 > 0$ with $x - 1 < 25$ gives $1 < x < 26$.
$\log_{5}(x - 2) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x - 2 < 5^{2} = 25$. Intersecting $x - 2 > 0$ with $x - 2 < 25$ gives $2 < x < 27$.

$\log_{5}(x + 1) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x + 1 < 5^{2} = 25$. Intersecting $x + 1 > 0$ with $x + 1 < 25$ gives $-1 < x < 24$.
$\log_{5}(x + 2) < 2$ needs the argument positive AND $x + 2 < 5^{2} = 25$. Intersecting $x + 2 > 0$ with $x + 2 < 25$ gives $-2 < x < 23$.

$\log_{2}(x - 1) > 2$ means $x - 1 > 2^{2} = 4$, i.e. $x > 5$ (the domain $x - 1 > 0$ is automatically satisfied).
$\log_{2}(x - 2) > 2$ means $x - 2 > 2^{2} = 4$, i.e. $x > 6$ (the domain $x - 2 > 0$ is automatically satisfied).
$\log_{2}(x - 3) > 2$ means $x - 3 > 2^{2} = 4$, i.e. $x > 7$ (the domain $x - 3 > 0$ is automatically satisfied).
$\log_{2}(x + 1) > 2$ means $x + 1 > 2^{2} = 4$, i.e. $x > 3$ (the domain $x + 1 > 0$ is automatically satisfied).
$\log_{2}(x + 2) > 2$ means $x + 2 > 2^{2} = 4$, i.e. $x > 2$ (the domain $x + 2 > 0$ is automatically satisfied).
$\log_{2}(x - 1) > 3$ means $x - 1 > 2^{3} = 8$, i.e. $x > 9$ (the domain $x - 1 > 0$ is automatically satisfied).

forms.title: Exponential inequalities, base < 1
forms.skill: A decreasing base flips the inequality when you compare exponents.

variants.explanation: Since the base $\tfrac{1}{2} < 1$ is decreasing, comparing $\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^x > \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{1}$ FLIPS the inequality: $x < 1$.
